benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 19-Jun official 3M $3,398.00, -$3.0 vs 18-Jun ($3,401.00). Front-end firm despite Hormuz reopening as the deficit holds. avg = May-2026 mean.

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 19-Jun cash $3,400 vs 3M $3,398 = +$2.0 = narrow BACKWARDATION (18-Jun was +$1.0). The 19-yr-high backwardation (~+$69 on 08-Jun) deflated to flat as the front-end risk premium unwound; the curve has now nudged back into mild backwardation, signalling the physical deficit still tightens the front despite the Gulf reopening.

macro_events: WAR PREMIUM FULLY UNWOUND — PRICE BASING ON THE DEFICIT FLOOR. Aluminium closed 19-Jun at ~$3,399 cash / ~$3,404 3M (+0.13% on the day), holding ~$3,400 rather than extending lower a week into the signed US-Iran deal. Westmetall's last official (17-Jun) put cash at $3,405.50 / 3M $3,410.00 (16-Jun cash $3,359.00 was the cycle low off the 02-Jun four-year high of $3,855). The structural-deficit floor is reasserting itself.

Deal holding: ~1 week on, the US naval blockade stays lifted and ships continue transiting the Strait of Hormuz (toll-free for two months, alongside a 60-day nuclear-talks window). Logistics relief is physically underway, though owners remain cautious on residual mine/drone risk.

Physical reality check: the deal restores the Gulf logistics path but not the damaged capacity. Middle East ~9–10% of global supply (~25% of ex-China) with ~3–3.2 Mt of capacity crippled; analysts see Gulf logistics normalizing within ~4–8 weeks but smelter production recovery gradual (input/power/restart lead-times). EGA's Al Taweelah needs ~12 mo to restore and ALBA remains partially suspended — the structural deficit persists.

Macro a headwind: the June FOMC signaled growing support for rate hikes on sticky inflation; the USD held firm (DXY ~99.9). Cross-asset confirmation from oil: Brent held ~$76 (Goldman $80 Q4 call) as the Hormuz reopening keeps the energy risk premium deflated — disinflationary and supportive of smelting economics, but reinforcing the softer-premium tone.

Ex-China supply pressure intact: China unwrought + semis exports ran +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May (Fastmarkets sees 2026 China exports +12–18%, potentially ~7.2 Mt) and Indonesian smelter output is rising; weak China data adds demand worry. The cash–3M curve holds a narrow, stable contango (cash ~$5 below 3M).
lme_commentary: Commentary: Friday 19-Jun officials (cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00; cash -$2.0, 3M -$3.0 on the day) are now confirmed by Westmetall and remain the latest print — Saturday 20 and Sunday 21-Jun are non-trading days, so there is no new session this run. Price based around $3,400 after the 16-Jun cycle low ($3,359) and well off the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). The cash–3M curve sits in a narrow backwardation (+$2.0) after fully unwinding the 19-year-high backwardation (~+$69 on 08-Jun); the persistence of mild backwardation signals the physical deficit still tightens the front even with a deal signed. Inventory at 315,300 t (-225 t on Friday, ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.4 days output) keeps drawing. The weekend news that the Hormuz reopening is conditional and slow (ADNOC: 'still not open'; ~230 tankers queued; UAE full-flow estimate 2027) supports the view that the structural-deficit floor — EGA ~12-month Al Taweelah restoration, Alba partially suspended, ~3 Mt removed — continues to cap downside.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$350–355/t FOB East Australia; Q2 oversupply pressuring

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region; easing on the building surplus

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$352/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer Platts API region (~$305/t); a ~1.2 Mt 2026 surplus is building on higher production/shipments into subdued demand.
Hormuz reopening (a week into the signed deal) is reversing the rerouting premium — alumina cargo flows through the Gulf are normalizing, easing the logistics distortion that had benefited China.

Bauxite: Guinea's oversupply pushed CIF China to ~$66/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34); Guinea is weighing export curbs (a >$100/dmt target) — a structural upside risk to raw-material cost independent of the Gulf truce.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply disruption that lifted regional refining costs should ease as the blockade lifts; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery may restart ahead of the smelter.
Net: input-cost picture skews softer post-deal (alumina ample, energy/logistics relief), though Guinea bauxite-curb risk keeps a floor under raw-material risk.
net_read: REGIME SHIFT INTACT — WAR PREMIUM UNWOUND, PRICE BASING ON THE STRUCTURAL FLOOR; REOPENING PROVING CONDITIONAL. Aluminium's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock (EGA Al Taweelah and Alba outages, Strait-of-Hormuz disruption, ~3 Mt removed). The signed US-Iran deal deflated essentially all of that war-risk premium, yet the market has based around $3,400 (Fri 19-Jun official cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00) off the 16-Jun cycle low ($3,359) rather than extending lower. No new LME session over the weekend (Sat 20 / Sun 21-Jun), so Friday's officials remain the latest print. The weekend's key development cuts against a clean unwind: the Hormuz reopening is proving conditional — ADNOC's Sultan Al Jaber said the Strait is 'still not open' as Iran restricts/conditions traffic, with ~230 loaded tankers queued inside the Gulf, and UAE officials see full flows not resuming until 2027. That slower-than-assumed normalization, alongside an unchanged physical shortfall (EGA ~12-month restoration, Alba partially down, LME stock 315,300 t, ~92% Russian-origin, drawing), reinforces the structural-deficit floor. The curve holds a narrow backwardation (cash +$2.0 vs 3M), signalling the front is still tight. US physical premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate.

feed.text: No new LME session (Sat/Sun). Friday 19-Jun officials (cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00; stock 315,300 t) remain the latest print. Weekend news: ADNOC's Sultan Al Jaber said the Strait of Hormuz is 'still not open' as Iran conditions/restricts traffic, with ~230 loaded tankers queued inside the Gulf; UAE sees full flows not resuming until 2027 — reopening slower than the market assumed.

Westmetall confirmed the 19-Jun LME officials: cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00 (cash -$2.0, 3M -$3.0 on the day); stock 315,300 t (-225 t). Price based around $3,400 rather than extending lower and the cash–3M curve held a narrow backwardation (+$2.0) — the structural deficit reasserting a tight front as the war premium fully unwound.
Brent held ~$76 (a week of softer oil) on receding Gulf risk; disinflationary and supportive of smelter power/refining costs, reinforcing the softer-premium tone.
LME inventory drew to ~316.5 kt (~92% Russian-origin), down ~38% YTD; available units stay scarce — the structural deficit is unaffected by diplomacy.

Analysts (Hellenic/Platts): Gulf logistics could approach pre-disruption pace within ~4–8 weeks once transit fully resumes, but smelter output recovery stays gradual on input/power/restart lead-times — near-term deficit persists. The conditional weekend reopening lengthens this timeline.

BofA (Widmer): aluminium 'vulnerable near-term' as supply risks ease — rising China output, potential Middle East inventory releases and new Indonesian supply compound downside. JPMorgan (Shearer): the 'major supply gap' remains until invisible inventories deplete.
June FOMC signalled growing support for rate hikes on sticky inflation — the USD held firm (DXY ~99.9), a headwind for dollar-priced metals.

news.headline: Friday 19-Jun LME official cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00 (both ~-0.1% on the day), stock 315,300 t (-225 t) — the latest print; no weekend session. The market based around $3,400 off the 16-Jun cycle low ($3,359) and down from the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855); cash–3M curve in a narrow backwardation (+$2.0).

Reopening proving conditional: ~10+ days into the signed US-Iran deal, ADNOC CEO Sultan Al Jaber says the Strait of Hormuz is 'still not open' as Iran restricts/conditions traffic, with ~230 loaded tankers queued inside the Gulf; UAE officials see full flows not resuming until 2027. Slower normalization than the market priced.
news.source: CNBC / Al Jazeera / Discovery Alert / ADNOC (Jun 2026)

news.headline: Reopening restores logistics, not capacity: Middle East ~9–10% of global supply (~25% ex-China), ~3 Mt crippled by the 28-Mar EGA/Alba strikes. EGA Al Taweelah ~12 months to restore; Alba partially suspended — structural deficit intact.
Brent held ~$76 a week into the deal; disinflationary and supportive of smelter costs but bearish for the physical premium. DXY firm near 99.9 on the FOMC hawkish lean.
June FOMC signaled support for rate hikes on sticky inflation — USD firm (DXY ~99.9). A headwind for dollar-priced metals layered on the premium unwind.
Cash–3M curve holds a narrow, stable ~$5 contango after the 19-yr-high backwardation (~$69 on 08-Jun) fully unwound — acute front-end scarcity eased, though total stocks stay low (~316.5kt).
LME stocks ~316.5kt (−38% YTD), ~1.4 days of global output; ~92% Russian-origin. Deficit unaffected by the deal; visible draws continuing.
China unwrought Al + semis exports +15.5% YoY to 632kt in May; Fastmarkets sees 2026 exports +12–18% (~7.2 Mt); BofA flags rising Indonesian output; weak China data dents demand.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián smelter restart completed Apr 2026. Q2 report due ~mid-Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due late Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 21-Jun (no new releases over the weekend). Q2 season begins mid/late-July: Alcoa confirmed 22-Jul (after close); Norsk Hydro late-Jul (has hedged ~67% of 2026 primary output at ~$3,000/t and ~51% of premiums at ~$571/t, net realized premium guided $530–580/t). Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com, Zawya/TradeArabia, GuruFocus.

commercial.text: With the deal a week old and the 19-Jun close holding ~$3,399 cash / ~$3,404 3M, the war premium is fully unwound but the price is basing — unrepaired Gulf capacity (EGA ~12 mo) and low LME stocks cap downside. Accelerate near-dated realization and ensure LME hedges are in place on priced tonnes; do NOT chase a top that has already broken, and do NOT over-hedge forward at distressed levels given the intact structural deficit. Maximise value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture while elevated.

The Gulf outage drove MJP Q2 +80% q/q (~$351.5) and record US MW premiums; Q3 negotiations open ~$250–380 depending on the Gulf-normalization pace (S&P/Fastmarkets), and CIF Japan/EDU are already easing. Lock in elevated premiums on renewals and spot allocations NOW, ahead of the ~4–8 week logistics-normalization giving way to fuller flows. As a non-disrupted supplier, capture premium share while EGA/ALBA remain impaired.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$352/t) easing on Q2 oversupply vs softer API region (~$305/t); the deal reverses the Hormuz rerouting premium. If net seller, lock firm ex-China pricing on remaining strength; if buyer for smelting, exploit softer spot windows as Gulf flows normalize.

A week into the deal, with the blockade lifted and ships transiting Hormuz, Gulf freight, war-risk insurance and routing risk are falling — but owners remain cautious on residual mine/drone risk, so normalization is progressing rather than complete. Keep contingency routing and demurrage clauses live through the 60-day window until flows are fully confirmed.

Manage the transition narrative with strategic accounts: physical tightness persists through the production-restart lag even as the geopolitical risk recedes and headline prices fall. Protect OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and convert reliability through the volatility into relationship equity and premium tolerance.

Track: (1) the durability of the base near $3,360–3,420 and any premium-unwind re-acceleration; (2) deal implementation — the CONDITIONAL Hormuz reopening (Iran restricting traffic, ~230 tankers queued, UAE full-flow estimate 2027), Strait transit pace and shipowner safe-passage confidence; (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines; (4) USD/Fed path after the hawkish June FOMC; (5) China export pace (Fastmarkets +12–18%), Indonesian ramp & Section 232 (50%); (6) Guinea bauxite-curb risk / alumina balance. The slower-than-assumed reopening modestly extends the premium-realization window — keep allocations flexible.

bottom_line: A regime shift that is stabilising, not collapsing. After the 16-Jun cycle low ($3,359) and a 17-Jun rebound, the geopolitical risk premium has fully deflated on the signed deal, yet Friday's 19-Jun official close held $3,400.00 cash / $3,398.00 3M (the latest print; no weekend session) as the structural deficit (EGA ~12-month restoration, Alba partial, ~92%-Russian LME stock drawing to 315,300 t) underpins a firm floor, with the cash–3M curve in a narrow backwardation (+$2.0). The weekend's signal is that reopening is conditional, not clean — Iran is restricting Hormuz traffic (~230 tankers queued; UAE sees full flows only by 2027) — which slows the bearish premium-normalization leg. The path remains a tug-of-war between Gulf logistics normalization (bearish premium) and ongoing invisible-inventory depletion plus a slower-than-assumed reopening (bullish). US physical premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate.
sources: London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (19-Jun official cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00 / stock 315,300 t confirmed; no weekend session 20–21 Jun)
Trading Economics / Westmetall (LME 3M 19-Jun: Al ~$3,400, Cu ~$13,595, Ni ~$17,580, Pb ~$1,954); Hellenic Shipping News; AlCircle
CNBC / Al Jazeera / Discovery Alert / ADNOC (deal signed 17-Jun; reopening conditional — Iran restricting traffic, ~230 tankers queued; UAE sees full flows by 2027)
Bank of America (Michael Widmer) & JPMorgan (Gregory Shearer) via Bloomberg/Mining.com
CNBC / OilPrice.com / Goldman Sachs (16–19 Jun-26: Brent ~$76–77; Q4 forecast $80)
Platts FOB Australia futures (~$307) & SMM/Fastmarkets/Alcircle; bauxite CIF China ~$66 (Discovery Alert/Fastmarkets)
S&P Global Platts / Fastmarkets (Q1 MJP $195; Q2 ~$351.5; Q3 open $250–380; US MW structurally elevated on 50% Section 232)
GAC China via SMM/ChemAnalyst; Fastmarkets; metal.com (SHFE ~CNY 24,100/t)
caveats: Report run Sun 21-Jun-2026 — a non-trading weekend day. There was no LME session on Sat 20 or Sun 21-Jun, so all LME, base-metal, FX and energy values are Friday 19-Jun closes / officials carried forward; they are the latest available, not stale, but will not refresh until the Monday session.

Westmetall's 18-Jun and 19-Jun officials are now confirmed and posted (19-Jun cash $3,400.00 / 3M $3,398.00 / stock 315,300 t). The benchmark, KPI and forward-path figures use these confirmed officials.

The US-Iran agreement is ~10+ days old; the weekend signal is that the Hormuz reopening is conditional — Iran is restricting/conditioning traffic (~230 tankers queued; ADNOC says the Strait is 'still not open'), and UAE officials see full flows only by 2027. Logistics normalization is slower than the market priced, and damaged Gulf capacity remains unrepaired.
Macro/FX/energy values are Friday point-in-time reads — oil (Brent ~$76 / WTI ~$73), DXY (~99.9) and gold (~$4,344) reflect 17–19 Jun; re-validate against live feeds at the Monday open.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus). Values shown are the most recent public anchors / status for the 2026 war-elevated regime — reconcile to the desk's licensed feed before pricing.
SHFE front-month is a last-reported reference (~CNY 24,100/t SMM spot, excl-VAT USD equivalent); verify on SMM / SHFE for the live print.
Alumina indices diverge by methodology (Fastmarkets/SMM FOB Australia vs Platts API region) — use the index contractually relevant to the trade.
This report is market intelligence, not trading or investment advice. Figures are point-in-time and should be re-validated before commercial commitments.

forward_path.basis: Confirmed Westmetall official; latest print (no weekend session). Market based ~$3,400 as the war premium fully unwound; structural-deficit floor holding.
Confirmed Westmetall official; -$3.0 on Friday. Curve in a narrow backwardation (cash +$2.0 vs 3M) — front still tight.
World Bank — eases as Gulf supply recovers

outlook.curve_note: Front-end backwardation flipped from a 19-yr high (~$69 on 08-Jun) to a narrow, stable contango (cash ~$5 below 3M on 19-Jun) — acute near-term scarcity eased, but consensus still sees a firm 2026 average before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply is repaired. The price basing ~$3,400 rather than breaking lower confirms the deficit floor.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.change: Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; exports seen +12–18% (Fastmarkets), potentially ~7.2 Mt
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; rising exports

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)

scenarios.drivers: Gulf restart slips, deficit deepens, China stays capped, USD softens
drivers_list: Supply: Gulf restart slips — EGA Al Taweelah / ALBA recovery runs beyond ~12 months, or fresh outages
Inventory: invisible stocks deplete and visible LME stocks draw below ~300kt — days-of-cover hits critical levels
China: output stays capped at 45 Mt; net exports fall as domestic demand absorbs metal
Demand: green-transition pull (EV, solar, grid) plus restocking accelerates
Macro: USD softens as the Fed turns dovish; risk-on flows into metals

scenarios.drivers: War premium unwinds (orderly) but structural deficit holds — consensus path
drivers_list: Supply: war premium unwinds (market based near $3,400 after the 16-Jun ~3-mo low) but Gulf capacity repairs only gradually (3–6 month lag)
Balance: structural deficit persists (~−365 kt 2026) with no fresh shocks
China: exports steady (~+12–18% YoY); output near the 45 Mt cap
Macro: USD firm after the hawkish June FOMC; Fed broadly on hold to one hike priced
Curve: the narrow contango holds — consensus $3,300–3,600 band intact

scenarios.drivers: Fast Gulf normalization, China + Indonesia supply surge, hawkish Fed/strong USD, demand softens
drivers_list: Geopolitics: fast, durable Gulf normalization — Hormuz fully reopens on schedule, restarts beat plan
Supply: China export surge plus rising Indonesian smelter output; Middle East inventories released
Macro: hawkish Fed / stronger USD; rate hikes weigh on dollar-priced metals

Inventory: visible stocks rebuild as supply returns; contango deepens

risks.risk: China + Indonesia supply (export pace, 45 Mt cap)

catalysts.event: China June trade & aluminium export data
Alcoa Q2 earnings (after close) — sector kickoff

ai_analysis: What changed: the war-premium unwind has completed and the price has based. After the 16-Jun cycle low (cash $3,359.00) and a 17-Jun rebound (official cash $3,405.50), the 19-Jun close firmed to ~$3,404 3M (+0.13%) — the market held ~$3,400 a week into the signed US-Iran deal (blockade lifted, ships transiting Hormuz) despite softer oil, signalling the structural-deficit floor.

Term structure: the cash–3M curve holds a narrow, stable ~$5 contango after the 19-yr-high backwardation (~$69 on 08-Jun) fully unwound — front-end scarcity eased, even as total LME stocks keep drawing (~316.5kt, −38% YTD).
Macro a headwind: the June FOMC's hawkish lean kept the USD firm (DXY ~99.9); Brent held ~$76 (Goldman $80 Q4 call) — disinflationary but reinforcing the softer-premium tone.

Sentiment skew: news flow stays net-bearish near-term (signed deal, premium unwind, softer oil, hawkish Fed, China +12–18% export ramp, BofA caution) — but the structural floor (JPM 'major supply gap'; ~4–8 week logistics lag, longer production-restart lag) has the price basing rather than breaking.

Forward bias unchanged: base case (50%) = a lower, choppier but supported price ($3,300–3,600 for 2026). Near-term watch is the Strait reopening pace and shipowner confidence; main upside risk = a slipped EGA/ALBA restart, main downside = fast Gulf normalization plus China/Indonesia supply.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): MJP Q2 settled near an 11-yr high (~$351.5/t, +80% q/q); CIF Japan spot easing (~$358) as Hormuz transit reopens. Q3 negotiations open ~$250–350 — the signed deal and physical Strait reopening now skew the range toward $260–320, with $340–350 only if Gulf restoration stalls (Platts/Fastmarkets).
European (EDU): ~$486/t, easing vs last month (~$519 avg) — demand-led and softening.
US Midwest: MW US Transaction ~$2,542/t (easing); duty-paid P1020A ~$2,162/t — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff, the slowest to normalize.

Common thread: premiums spiked on the Gulf supply outage and are now rolling over a week into the signed deal; the actual Strait reopening pace is the key downside catalyst into H2-26, while unrepaired Gulf capacity keeps a floor.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$66 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$34); oversupply has roughly halved prices since Jan-25 — Guinea weighing export curbs toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Fastmarkets/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$352/t) easing on Q2 oversupply; API region softer (~$305; FOB futures ~$307). Surplus building into H2.
Bauxite: Guinea oversupply pushed CIF China to ~$66/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34) — but Guinea's export-curb threat (a >$100/dmt target) is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.
Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite but caustic keeps a floor.
Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-traded — full transparency (3M ~$13,800/t, ~$6.34/lb, steadied on strong China data; COMEX near records on US tariff premium); alloy input for 2xxx & some 6xxx

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Bulk-freight proxy for bauxite/alumina; ~2,600 (19-Jun), near a multi-month low; Capesize BCI ~3,800 — Trading Economics / HandyBulk / Bloomberg

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports & ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz; ~10+ days into the signed US-Iran deal the Strait is still NOT fully open — ADNOC CEO Al Jaber says traffic remains conditioned/restricted by Iran, ~230 loaded tankers queued inside the Gulf, and UAE officials see full flows only by 2027. Normalization is slower than the market assumed — keeps the premium-unwind gradual.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: Unwinding a week into the signed deal; keep cover live through the 60-day window as residual risk lingers.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround during closure — costly contingency; relief as Hormuz normalizes

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live until routing normalises

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — watch PPAs/carbon), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, ~$13,800/t).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics is the swing factor now: a week into the Hormuz reopening, freight and war-risk surcharges are unwinding — but keep contingency routing and cover live until normalisation is confirmed; BDI near a 2026-cycle low (~2,600) eases bulk costs.

Procurement read: power + carbon (coke/pitch) drive roughly half of conversion cost — prioritise these for hedging / forward contracting; alloying metals are smaller line items but China-concentrated.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): a five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) completed at the $3,855 four-year high in early June 2026 — the final leg a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off (commodities typically extend the fifth), confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence. An A-B-C correction is underway: wave A bottomed at the 16-Jun $3,359 cycle low, and price has since recovered to and held $3,400 (Fri 19-Jun official; no weekend session) — a developing wave-B bounce, with the weekend's conditional Hormuz reopening (Iran restricting traffic, ~230 tankers queued) helping the deficit floor hold. Base case (~50%): wave B stalls in the $3,490–$3,540 band (23.6–38.2% of wave A), then wave C resumes lower into the $3,260–$3,080 confluence (38.2–50% retracement plus the Q1-26 fourth-wave-of-lesser-degree base) before the next higher-degree advance. Bull alternate (~35%, modestly firmer this run on slower reopening): $3,855 was wave 3 and the 16-Jun low completed wave 4 — valid only above ~$3,000, then new highs toward $3,900+. The bearish-primary count is invalidated on a sustained weekly close back above $3,855; a decisive break below ~$2,894 (61.8%) opens a deeper correction. Medium confidence; counts held — no invalidation level breached this run.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Short-term (daily): the decline off $3,855 unfolded as wave A into the 16-Jun $3,359 cash low; price rebounded to and is holding $3,400 (Fri 19-Jun official cash $3,400.00) — a developing wave-B bounce, with markets closed over the weekend so Friday's print stands. The conditional Hormuz reopening flagged over the weekend (ADNOC: 'still not open') argues the bearish premium-unwind is slower than assumed, supporting the held $3,400 base. Expect wave B to stall in the $3,490–$3,540 band (23.6–38.2% of the drop), then wave C lower toward the $3,260 zone that anchors the long-term 4th-of-lesser-degree/.382–.50 confluence. Alternation favours a shallow, choppy B (flat/triangle) given A was a sharp 5-wave decline. Bullish invalidation: a daily close back above $3,540 opens a retest of the $3,700s and argues the $3,359 low was a completed correction. Bearish invalidation of the floor: a sustained break under $3,260 targets $3,150. Confidence: moderate — the flat front structure and held $3,400 base keep the B-wave count primary.

premium_drivers.d: Gulf regional production −63% YoY; reopening is CONDITIONAL — Iran restricting Hormuz traffic (~230 tankers queued; UAE sees full flows by 2027), so premiums and aluminium freight ease only slowly.
50% tariff on full customs value (eff. 8-Jun-2026, through 2027) → US Midwest at a record ~105¢/lb.
EU import duty reverted to 6% (from 4%, 1-Jan-2026) plus CBAM → European premiums rising.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
